selection: Choose by smell, not looks. A ripe one is fragrant at the stem and gives slightly when cradled; heavy for its size means juicy.
Look for taut, split-free skins and deep even colour. Give the punnet a sniff, it should smell sweet.
Heavy and faintly soft, with a strong scent at the stem. The shoulders should be fully coloured, not pale.
Firm, richly red, with a sweet stem smell. A little give is fine if it is bound for sauce.

notes: recoloured variant of the tomato print
selection: Firm and glossy is the point. Choose unblemished ones with taut skin for frying or chutney.
Light for its size with tight, shiny skin. Press gently, it should spring back; a lasting dent means old and bitter.
Small and firm beats big. Taut glossy skin and a dense heft; oversized ones turn seedy and watery.
Firm with unblemished skin. The smaller marrows are sweeter and less watery.
Firm end to end with no soft or wrinkled spots. Slimmer ones tend to have fewer seeds.
Heavy, glossy and firm with taut skin and a fresh green stem. Wrinkles or soft patches mean it is past it.
Firm, glossy and taut. Wrinkled skin means age, though it still cooks fine if you want it milder.
Small, firm and bright green with smooth skin. The little ones are mild, the plump ones can surprise you.
Choose ones that fill their papery husk and feel firm; the husk should be fresh and pale, not dry and brown.
Firm all over with dry, papery skin, no green shoots or soft necks. It should feel heavy and smell only faintly.
Firm and heavy with tight, dry skin. Avoid softness at the top or a strong smell through the skin.
Firm and dry with intact skin, no sprouting from the tip. Heavy for its size is best.
Crisp white bulbs and perky, bright green tops. Skip any that are slimy or yellowing at the leaf.
Firm white shanks and fresh green tops. Slimmer leeks are more tender; avoid split or yellowing leaves.
Plump, firm heads with tight papery skin. Squeeze gently, it should feel solid with no soft or sprouting cloves.
Large firm bulbs with tight skin and no give when pressed. Milder than regular garlic, so pick the biggest sound one.
Bright, perky leaves with a strong garlicky smell when rubbed. Skip anything wilted or yellowing.
Firm and smooth with bright colour. If the tops are on they should be fresh and green, not limp.
Firm with vivid colour and smooth skin. Fresh feathery tops mean a recent pull.
Firm, smooth and heavy for its size. Small to medium are sweeter; fresh green tops mean recently harvested.

notes: recoloured variant of the beetroot print
selection: Firm and smooth with unbroken skin. Smaller roots are sweeter and less woody.
Firm with taut skin and bright colour. Perky leaves mean fresh; soft, spongy roots are old.
Firm and crisp with smooth skin and lively tops. A gentle squeeze should meet no give.

notes: big white root; no matching print — name-card fallback
selection: Firm and heavy with smooth, taut skin and no soft spots. It should feel dense, not hollow.

notes: white/purple root; no matching print — name-card fallback
selection: Small, firm and heavy with smooth skin. Larger turnips turn woody and sharp.

notes: yellow/purple root; no matching print — name-card fallback
selection: Heavy and firm with smooth skin, no soft patches. Smaller ones are sweeter and less fibrous.

notes: recoloured variant of the carrot print
selection: Firm and smooth, small to medium. Very large parsnips have a woody core worth avoiding.
Heavy and firm with as few knobbles as possible. A light, hollow one will be pithy inside.
Firm with taut skin, no green tinge or sprouting eyes. Green means bitter, so pass it by.

notes: maps to the potato archetype print
selection: Firm with thin, flaky skin that rubs off easily; that thin skin is the sign of a true new potato.
Firm and smooth with no soft spots, cracks, or sprouting. Even-shaped ones cook more evenly.
Firm and knobbly with no soft or wrinkled patches. Smoother tubers are simply easier to peel.
Firm bulbs smaller than a tennis ball, with fresh leaves. Big ones go woody.
Firm, heavy roots with no soft or mouldy spots. It should smell sharp only once cut.
Heavy and tight-headed for its size, with crisp outer leaves. Loose or yellowing leaves mean age.
Crisp, deeply crinkled leaves and a head that feels heavy. Avoid limp or browning outer leaves.
Firm and heavy for its size with fresh, tight leaves. A pale, loose head is older.
Bright, perky leaves with no yellowing or slime. The fresher they look, the sweeter they cook.
Dense and heavy with tight, glossy leaves and no splits. It should feel solid when squeezed.
Tight, creamy-white curds with no grey speckling, wrapped in fresh green leaves.
Bright lime spirals, tight and unbroken, with fresh leaves. Skip any browning tips.
Tight, dark blue-green heads with firm stalks. Yellowing florets or open buds mean it is going over.
Perky purple spears with tight buds and no yellow flowering. Snappable stalks are freshest.
Firm, crisp stems and tight green heads. Limp stems or open buds mean age.
Tight, firm, bright green sprouts, on the stalk if you can. Loose or yellowing leaves mean old.
Deeply coloured, perky leaves with firm stems. Yellowing or wilting means past its best.
Dark, firm, blistered leaves that stand up. Limp or yellow-edged leaves are old.
Crisp white stems and glossy green leaves, no slime or yellowing at the base.
Crisp, upright stems and glossy, unblemished leaves. Wilted leaves or slimy stalks mean old.
Bright, springy leaves with no dark slimy patches. It should smell fresh and green.
Perky, deep green leaves with no yellowing or sliminess. Smaller leaves are milder.
A head that feels heavy for its size, with soft, unbruised leaves and no brown edges.

notes: maps to the lettuce archetype print
selection: Crisp, tightly packed leaves and firm ribs. Wilting outer leaves or brown edges mean age.

notes: maps to the lettuce archetype print
selection: Small, tight, heavy heads with crisp pale hearts. Avoid browning at the base.

notes: maps to the lettuce archetype print
selection: Crisp, springy leaves with pale hearts. Limp or browning frizz means old stock.
Firm, heavy heads with tight, glossy crimson leaves and a crisp white core.
Deep green, perky leaves and firm stems with a peppery smell. Yellowing bunches are old.

notes: maps to the lettuce archetype print
selection: Small, dark, springy rosettes with no yellowing or slime. Delicate, so buy fresh and use fast.
White, tight bulbs with crisp feathery fronds and no browning. Rounder bulbs are more tender.
Firm, crisp stalks that snap cleanly, with fresh green tops. Limp, bendy stalks mean age.
Firm spears with tight, closed tips and squeaky stems. Cut ends should look moist, not dried out.
Firm, crisp, glossy stalks that snap rather than bend. Thinner stalks are less stringy.
Heavy, tight heads whose leaves squeak when squeezed. Loose, dry leaves mean old.
Firm, pale stalks with fresh cut ends and no browning. It should feel crisp, not rubbery.
Bright green, crisp and springy with a salty-sea smell. Skip any that is soft or yellowing.
Heavy for its size with a hard, dull rind and a firm stalk attached. A hollow sound means ripe.
Matte, hard skin and a heavy feel, an even beige colour. A green tinge means underripe.
Hard, dull skin and heavy in the hand; a shiny skin means picked early. A patch of orange is fine.
Firm and heavy with hard blue-grey skin and an intact stalk. Dense weight means good flesh.
Hard, pale-yellow skin with no soft spots or green. Heavy for its size is best.
Hard, dull deep-green skin and a heavy, dense feel. A firm stalk is a good sign.
Slim, firm beans that snap crisply with bright colour. Bulging or limp pods mean old and tough.

notes: maps to the pods archetype print
selection: Firm, bright pods that snap cleanly, with no seeds bulging through. Smaller ones are more tender.

notes: maps to the pods archetype print
selection: Plump, firm, bright green pods. Smaller pods hold younger, sweeter beans; leathery pods are old.

notes: maps to the pods archetype print
selection: Full, firm pods streaked crimson, with beans you can feel inside. Limp pods are past their best.

notes: maps to the pods archetype print
selection: Plump, firm, bright pods that feel full. A pod should squeak and snap; dull or yellow pods are old.

notes: maps to the pods archetype print
selection: Flat, crisp, bright pods with tiny peas inside. They should snap cleanly, not bend.

notes: maps to the pods archetype print
selection: Plump, crisp, bright pods that snap. Soft or dull ones are past it.
Bright green, firm, fuzzy pods with beans you can feel. Yellowing pods are old.
Fresh green husks and golden, slightly sticky silks. Peel back a little; kernels should be plump and milky.

notes: maps to the sweetcorn archetype print
selection: Firm, pale, unblemished cobs. The fresher and crisper, the sweeter.
Firm, dry caps with a good smell and closed gills. Slimy or wrinkled means old.
Firm, dry, closed caps with no dark or slimy spots. A clean earthy smell is a good sign.
Firm caps that feel dry, gills intact and not blackened. A slippery cap means old.
Firm, moist-looking but not slimy, with intact fan edges. Avoid yellowing or dried-out edges.
Firm, dry, bright golden with a fruity smell. Skip any slimy, dark, or shrivelled ones.
Firm, heavy caps with a pale spongy underside and a rich smell. Soft or wormy stems mean pass.
Firm, dry, plump caps with curled edges. Cracked, dry, or slimy caps are past it.
Perky, deep green bunches with a fresh smell and no yellowing or wilt.
Bright, springy leaves with a strong citrusy smell. Yellowing or slimy bunches are old.
Bright, unblemished leaves with a strong scent. Black spots or wilting mean it is fading fast.
Vivid, perky leaves that smell strongly when rubbed. Skip darkening or limp stems.
Feathery, bright green fronds with a fresh smell, not wilted or yellowing.
Delicate, perky bright leaves with no wilt. Fragile, so use it soon after buying.
Glossy, springy leaves with a strong aniseed smell. Blackened leaves mean old.
Firm, upright, bright green stems with no yellowing or slime at the tips.
Firm sprigs with pliable needles and a strong scent. Dry, dropping needles mean old.
Fresh, fragrant sprigs with tiny firm leaves that cling. Bare, brittle stems are past it.
Firm, velvety, silver-green leaves with a strong smell. Wilting or dark spots mean age.
Fragrant, firm sprigs with even green leaves. Skip any that look dry or yellowed.
Glossy, firm, deep green leaves with a strong scent when torn. Fresh beats dusty and brittle.
Small, soft, fragrant leaves on firm stems, no wilting or yellowing.
Firm, heavy stalks with a fresh lemon smell and pale, tight bases. Dry, woody stalks are old.
Firm and heavy with taut, unbruised skin. Flick it, a crisp sound is good; it should smell faintly sweet.
Firm, heavy and unbruised with taut green skin. Size is fine here, but no soft brown spots.
Firm with a slight give at the neck when ripe. Buy firm and let it soften at home over a day or two.
Firm with a little give at the stem end; the skin bronzes as it ripens. Ripen hard ones on the counter.
Firm, heavy and fragrant, with golden fuzz. A strong perfume means ripe; hard green ones ripen at home.
Firm and brown; medlars are eaten bletted, so buy firm and let them soften indoors before eating.
Plump with a slight give and a dusty bloom on the skin. Rock-hard is underripe, mushy is over.
Small, firm, deep blue-black with a bloom. Tart by nature, so choose firm ones for cooking.

notes: recoloured variant of the plum print
selection: Plump with a slight give and a green-gold colour. A honeyed smell means ripe and sweet.
Firm, glossy, plump, with fresh green stems attached. Dull skin or dry stems mean old.
Press gently by the stem, it should just yield, and smell it. Loudly sweet means ripe; hard means picked early.

notes: maps to the stone-fruit archetype print
selection: A slight give along the seam and a sweet smell. Firm ones ripen on the counter in a day or two.

notes: maps to the stone-fruit archetype print
selection: Plump, fragrant and evenly orange with a slight give. Pale or hard ones were picked too soon.
Soft, heavy and hanging slightly, with a sweet smell and maybe a bead of syrup at the base. Firm figs never sweeten.

notes: recoloured variant of the grapes print
selection: Plump grapes firmly attached to green, pliable stems, with a fresh bloom. Brown stems mean old.
Deeply coloured, plump grapes that hold firm on green stems. Wrinkled or dropping grapes are old.
Fully red to the tip with fresh green caps and a strong sweet smell. White shoulders mean picked early.

notes: maps to the berries archetype print
selection: Plump, dry, evenly coloured berries with no mould in the punnet; sniff for sweetness.

notes: maps to the berries archetype print
selection: Deep black, plump and glossy; red beads mean underripe and sour. Check the base for mould.

notes: maps to the berries archetype print
selection: Firm, dusty-blue berries that roll freely, with a silvery bloom. Soft, leaky ones spoil the box.

notes: maps to the berries archetype print
selection: Glossy, plump strings of bright red berries firmly on the stem, no shrivelling.

notes: maps to the berries archetype print
selection: Firm, glossy, deep-black berries on the strig, with no splitting or wrinkling.

notes: maps to the berries archetype print
selection: Firm, taut berries; hard and green for cooking, softer and blushed for eating raw.
Heavy for its size with taut, glossy skin; that weight means juicy. Hard, light lemons are dry.

notes: maps to the citrus archetype print
selection: Heavy and firm with glossy, deep green skin. A little give and a fragrant skin mean juicy.

notes: maps to the citrus archetype print
selection: Heavy for its size with firm, taut skin. Weight matters more than colour for juiciness.

notes: maps to the citrus archetype print
selection: Heavy and firm; a reddish blush hints at the colour inside, but weight is the real tell.

notes: maps to the citrus archetype print
selection: Heavy for the size with glossy skin, slightly loose is fine. Light, puffy fruit is dry inside.

notes: maps to the citrus archetype print
selection: Heavy and firm with springy, taut skin. The heavier it feels, the juicier.
Sniff the blossom end for a sweet musky smell; it should feel heavy with a slight give there.

notes: maps to the melon archetype print
selection: Heavy for its size with a creamy-yellow ground spot; a deep hollow knock means ripe.

notes: maps to the melon archetype print
selection: Heavy, with waxy (not shiny) skin, a faint sweet smell, and slight give at the blossom end.
Heavy for its size with taut, leathery skin; the heaviest are juiciest. A little angular is fine.
For the soft type, buy jelly-soft and deep orange; for the crisp type, firm and unblemished.
It should give slightly to a gentle press when ripe. Buy firm and ripen at home; rock-hard is sour.
Cradle, do not poke; it should yield gently all over. Firm ones ripen in a few days on the counter.
Heavy and fragrant at the base, with fresh green leaves; a leaf should tug free with a little resistance.
Plump, glossy and slightly wrinkled, with no sugar crystallising out and no dryness. Soft means fresh.
Firm with a slight give and even colour; handle carefully for the tiny spines. Too soft means over.
Papery husks that are dry and intact, with a firm, golden berry inside.
Firm, heavy roots with smooth, taut skin. Wrinkled or soft ginger is old and fibrous.
For loose olives, look for firm, glossy fruit in clean brine; they should smell fresh, not musty.
